text: I do! But I already bought into Typed FP, and was blown away by this talk. "Tell more things about your program to the compiler and it'll do amazing things!"

I'm confused by the word 'comments' here..
I'm confused by the word 'comments' here..
human readable sentences in code to explain to humans what the code is doing
human readable sentences in code to explain to humans what the code is doing

So all comments are automatic trace statements.  That might teach people to avoid bad comment habits (like commenting out instead of deleting or wrapping in #define if your language supports that) but it also assumes that many comments are useful runtime information, which probably isn't true.  Basically I'm not sure why it's better or more clear than explicit traces, which can display dynamic runtime information and can also be read like comments when the code isn't running

that's the idea, make the comments useful so they are kept up to date and provide information. Then they become "explicit traces"
that's the idea, make the comments useful so they are kept up to date and provide information. Then they become "explicit traces"
also, you can turn them on to stdout to do print based debugging :stuck_out_tongue:

Wow, so you _are_ saying what I thought you were saying :smile: My mind reels. What's wrong with print statements? Why conflate categories? Is every comment I may write useful as a trace?

In my code I have prints and traces and _4_ kinds of comments, so I'm going in the opposite direction.

everybody "complains" that comments get out of date because they don't run, they would be less outdated if they where used for something

you could add tags to comments and trace some but not others, and categorize them the way you like. Since they have to make sense in sequence (to make sense in a trace) you could see them as half way to literate programming

using the ast you can build them into "documentation" by splitting different paths in ifs into alternatives

just because someone sees something in a log that is out of date or incorrect doesn't necessarily mean they'll fix it, or know that its wrong, or that it matches what the code is actually doing

your assumption is that comments go out of date because people aren't reading them, and if they are part of a trace they will?
your assumption is that comments go out of date because people aren't reading them, and if they are part of a trace they will?
the issue with comments going out of date is they are taken at face value (because its a nice cliff notes version of the code) and the code associated with them is not checked

Reminds me of setting verbosity levels in unix utils.  Best part is, getting this in $favorite_lang is one find-replace in your build step away.

<@UBN9AFS0N> you could use tags to distinguish traceable comments from others, yes. But then you have the same issues as before with untraceable comments. And it's not clear what making tracing code look like comments buys you..

<@UBN9AFS0N> you could use tags to distinguish traceable comments from others, yes. But then you have the same issues as before with untraceable comments. And it's not clear what making tracing code look like comments buys you..
There _is_ some precedent for running code in comments: doctests. But running production code from comments seems fraught.
There _is_ some precedent for running code in comments: doctests. But running production code from comments seems fraught.
I do check tracing statements in my tests, so I'm sympathetic to this line of thinking..
I do check tracing statements in my tests, so I'm sympathetic to this line of thinking..

I guess you could have a system where programmers link comments to code blocks and automatically flags\warns about comments that haven't been approved and\or updated for edited code.  Might be a good feature for a code review system.  At some point unless you have an AI in the compiler that can fully understand comments and flag comments as potentially\wrong or out of date there is going to be no perfect system.  An extreme version would be write comments in some higher level psuedo code that can actually be executed and match it with the runtime version of the code. I think that would only be useful in certain niche situations though.  In games\game engines that isn't totally uncommon, a lot of the time you have a slower, C++ version of a function as a reference and a optimized version using assembly or SIMD intrinsics that is harder to read.

I rather dislike Haskell and Idris. I find the notation obfuscating, inelegant, and cumbersome. The whole point of types is to prevent errors, but they get so concerned with type checking that they forget it is about expression. If you wanted to declare a pointer to a pointer of an array of records, Modula-2 offered that in the 70's, with the ability to turn off type checking when you needed to break the rules for some low-level purpose. I wish people would read Prof. Wirth's work, or spend 10 minutes with modula-2 to see a prior high-water mark in modularization and type protection.  The majority of errors in graphical interactive programming nowadays are related to drawing and event handling, and many languages have neither a database nor a graphical model in the language, so for my purposes of the future of coding, they represent the past of terminal-based languages that are actually retrograde in their effect. Sorry to be such a wet blanket on this line, but you're talking to a guy who heard John Backus in person in 1973 talk about his functional language. FP is not new, and one should be cautious about thinking FP is a magical cure. We all want composability, and reusable parts.  I believe there are only 2 unsolved problems in computer science: 1) how to achieve re-usable parts, and 2) how to make debugging go down from 85% of the effort to a more modest fraction.

Hi <@UF71PR58X> - welcome! While I don't consider myself in the 'typed FP' camp, FP definitely comes up in this slack and spurs interest - just search for Elm, Hazel, Lamdu or even 'FP'. So it is not out of place at all.
As for Edward's response - I don't think it is appropriate or conducive to good discussion. There are much better ways to disagree.

here’s a thought that crossed my mind. someone could parse the entire hackage and look for all the type transitions. maybe they could discover some interesting patterns. maybe this could be fed to a machine learning algorithm that would then suggest patterns while you code
